items.q: Does the limit of f(x) as x approaches a have to equal f(a)?
items.correct: No — the limit can exist even if f(a) is undefined or different from the limit.
items.misconception: The limit is always just the value of f at that point — that's how you compute it.
items.random_wrong: Limits always equal zero unless the function is constant.
items.why: Students learn limits by substituting (for continuous functions) and don't realise limits and function values are conceptually distinct.

items.correct: No — a prime number has exactly two distinct factors (1 and itself); 1 has only one factor.
items.misconception: Yes — 1 has no factors except itself, which makes it prime.
items.random_wrong: Yes — 1 is the smallest prime number.
items.why: Students apply a loose 'only divisible by 1 and itself' rule and miss the precise requirement of exactly two distinct factors.

items.q: What does the graph of y = 1/x do as x approaches 0?
items.correct: y goes to plus or minus infinity — the graph has a vertical asymptote at x = 0.
items.misconception: y equals 0 because dividing 1 by something small is small.
items.random_wrong: y equals 1 because the numerator stays the same.
items.why: Students confuse 'small denominator gives small result' (true for normal fractions) with 'small denominator gives huge result' (true for 1/x near 0).
Correct answer: np. “n(1-p)” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Normal distribution. “Poisson” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The real figure is 5. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
